title: Is Sanskrit Week Intended to Create Controversy?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's language-policy argument, historical accounts, attributed quotations, dates, and paragraph order. The Tamil reading copy below restores the source-printed opening salutation, removes one full OCR-junk line, and corrects clear OCR character and word-boundary errors encountered during translation. The canonical source document remains unchanged. Historical linguistic claims are presented as the cited authors' claims, not as independent conclusions by the translator. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: A few weeks ago, the Union government instructed officials to use Hindi on social media. On 17 June 2014, *The Economic Times* published a lengthy article headlined: "Order Issued in Accordance with Prime Minister Narendra Modi's Priority: Union Home Ministry Asks Government Officials to Use Hindi on Social Media." I immediately issued a statement saying that everyone concerned for the country's welfare wished and requested that the Prime Minister attend to the matter.

After my statement, the Prime Minister's Office said that Hindi would be used as the language of communication on social media only in Hindi-speaking States and that there would be no change in non-Hindi-speaking States. Union Minister Venkaiah Naidu, then visiting Tamil Nadu, also told reporters: "A language cannot be imposed upon the people of any region. The BJP's position is that Hindi should not be made the sole official language on social media, and that every regional language and mother tongue must be given due importance." I have already reported these details.

Later, a report dated 30 June 2014 said that Hindi was becoming compulsory in Union government offices in the southern States; that eleven employees who did not attend Hindi training or take the examination had been issued memoranda; and that the entry of Hindi into attendance registers and Government Orders had caused anxiety among Union government employees.

Now the director of the Central Board of Secondary Education, the CBSE, has sent a four-page circular directing schools to observe the second week of August as Sanskrit Week. Linguists have pointed out that this order, issued on 30 June, applies even to regions where people do not know what Sanskrit is.

The circular states that the CBSE is committed to teaching and learning Sanskrit. It declares that Sanskrit is the taproot of Indian culture and contains India's treasury of knowledge. It directs schools to conduct essay competitions and similar programmes to popularise Sanskrit and to strengthen awareness of the language among students.

In August 2001, during the previous National Democratic Alliance government, Human Resource Development Minister Murli Manohar Joshi directed that the first week of August be observed annually as Sanskrit Week. Linguists believe the present effort, like that of 2001, is intended to plant in students the idea that Sanskrit is the mother of every Indian language.

Just as we cannot accept the imposition of Hindi, we cannot accept the imposition of Sanskrit. Other parties in Tamil Nadu have also condemned the proposed Sanskrit Week. The CPI(M) has strongly opposed it and demanded that the Union government withdraw the measure immediately.
While writing about Sanskrit, I wish to share material I recently read under the heading "Tamil, the World's First Language" in Ko. Velnambi's book *Anjiraith Thumbi: Silamboli S. Chellappan*.

The German missionary Ziegenbalg lived and carried out religious service at Tharangambadi in Tamil Nadu. When returning to his homeland, he travelled through London. The Archbishop of Canterbury hosted a reception for him and proudly announced that a citation honouring his distinguished service in Tamil Nadu would be read in Latin, a great language.

Accepting the welcome, Ziegenbalg replied: "You said that the citation welcoming me would be read in the exalted language of Latin. It is only fitting that I too should express my thanks in an exalted language." He then spoke in Tamil. When those present asked the language's name, Ziegenbalg proudly answered: "Tamil." That is one account.

On another occasion, Vadalur Vallalar Ramalinga Adigal and the Kanchi Sankaracharya of his time met. The Sankaracharya reportedly told Vallalar: "I have read your hymns. Tamil is a fine language; nevertheless, Sanskrit is the mother of every language in the world." Vallalar immediately replied: "If Sanskrit is the mother tongue, Tamil is the father tongue." Even if there is a mother, how can there be birth without a father? Vallalar thus maintained that Tamil was the first cause from which the world's languages emerged.

Parithimar Kalaignar, father of the Pure Tamil movement, declared with upright scholarly pride: "We will not sell classical Tamil to anyone for money." In his *History of the Tamil Language*, he wrote: "Researchers conclude that the alphabet of the Aryan language was fashioned after the Tamil system. Aryans have a practice of creating new scripts to suit the places to which they travel. Following the Tamil script, they created a new script called Grantha."

In his invocation to Tamil in *Manonmaniam*, Sundaram Pillai praised its undiminished youth: "Unlike Aryan speech, whose worldly usage declined and disappeared, we marvel at your enduring youthful excellence, forget ourselves and praise you."

In *History of Tamil*, Devaneya Pavanar wrote: "The world's languages are estimated at nearly three thousand. Among them, Tamil alone unites antiquity, primacy, simplicity, luminosity, youth, richness, motherhood, purity, refinement, threefold excellence, sweetness, individuality, greatness, auspiciousness, naturalness and universality." No one can simply deny these statements.

When it was proposed under British rule that Sanskrit should replace mother-tongue education and become the common medium throughout India, supposedly uniting Indians from Kanyakumari to Kashmir, support and opposition arose across the country. Tamil scholars raised their voices against it. Parithimar Kalaignar and Professor M. C. Purnalingam Pillai worked vigorously against making Sanskrit the mother-tongue subject.

In his 1856 *Comparative Grammar of the Dravidian or South-Indian Family of Languages*, Caldwell wrote that Tamil, established as the uniquely refined classical language among the Dravidian languages, could eliminate every Sanskrit word that had entered it, continue to live, and grow in richness without depending on them even slightly.

In his *History of the Tamil Language*, Parithimar Kalaignar explained that a classical language is one possessing cultivated character, mature civilisation and purity, and that Tamil clearly satisfied that definition.

The debate eventually became whether college curricula should teach each student's mother tongue or Sanskrit, then recognised as India's only classical language. Viceroy Lord Curzon decided to entrust the matter to the University of Madras, then among India's most renowned universities.

Because the Viceroy's request concerned language, the Vice-Chancellor considered the University Teachers' Association the appropriate body and entrusted it with the decision. On learning this, Parithimar Kalaignar and M. C. Purnalingam Pillai met every member of the association's executive committee individually and stressed the necessity of mother-tongue education. The two worked tirelessly.

As they hoped, the association resolved that every student's own mother tongue should be taught in colleges and sent the resolution to the Vice-Chancellor. He forwarded it to Lord Curzon as the University of Madras's decision. Curzon accepted it as government policy and provided for college students throughout India to study their respective mother tongues. Mother-tongue education was thereby secured as a college subject for India's citizens.

Tamil people must also remember with gratitude that Pandithurai Thevar, founder of the Fourth Tamil Sangam at Madurai, provided powerful support behind the efforts of Parithimar Kalaignar and M. C. Purnalingam Pillai. My dear friend Manavai Mustafa set out all these explanations in his book *Classical Language: Within and Without*.

Even my dear friend L. Ganesan, a member of the BJP National Executive, has said that observing Sanskrit Week would not harm Tamil or Tamil Nadu; that the words "All India" and "Dravidian" in the AIADMK's name are Sanskrit words; and that opposition to Sanskrit is inappropriate. At the same time, he suggested observing the first seven days of the Tamil month of Thai as Tamil Language Week for classical Tamil. By his own argument, however, celebrating Sanskrit Week would create opportunities for still more Sanskrit words to enter Tamil.

It was reported that Chief Minister Jayalalithaa wrote to the Prime Minister opposing Sanskrit Week and asking that each State celebrate a language week based on its own linguistic culture, with Classical Tamil Week in Tamil Nadu and the relevant language week elsewhere.

Yet on 8 July 2014, from the Secretariat, the same Jayalalithaa inaugurated by video link the Vivekananda Cultural Centre built by the Ramakrishna Math in the Vivekananda House campus on Kamarajar Salai, with financial assistance of two crore rupees from the Tamil Nadu government.

The government's press release said that the 8,000-square-foot centre, constructed for Vivekananda's 150th birth anniversary, would undertake cultural-development activities including teaching Tamil, Sanskrit, English and Hindi. In other words, the Tamil Nadu government provided two crore rupees, and the Chief Minister herself opened a centre whose work includes teaching Sanskrit and Hindi.

The people of Tamil Nadu must consider what kind of double role this is: the State gives two crore rupees to a cultural centre near the Secretariat in the capital that will teach Hindi and Sanskrit, while the Chief Minister writes to the Union government saying that Sanskrit Week must not be celebrated in Tamil Nadu.

As far as we are concerned, we have never accepted the imposition of Hindi or Sanskrit upon any section of the people. We cannot accept Sanskrit Week in CBSE schools in Tamil Nadu. We strongly condemn and oppose this Union government measure.

When controversy arose over the Hindi social-media instruction, the Union government clarified that it applied only to Hindi-speaking States. In the same manner, it must immediately issue an appropriate corrective statement on Sanskrit Week.

The Modi government should abandon the foundational argument of Saraswati and Sanskrit and, as promised during the election, concentrate upon the livelihood problems of India's people. That would do far more to avoid contradictions and conflicts of opinion.
With affection,
M. K.
22 July 2014
